calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Prefiling Order And Remove From Judicial Council Vexatious Litigant List
Off Calendar Feb-03-2023 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF TINA YAN's Motion To Vacate Prefiling Order And Remove From Judicial Council Vexatious Litigant List.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion to vacate the prefiling order and remove plaintiff from the vexatious litigant list is denied.

Even though the court dismissed this action on 10/24/22, it has jurisdiction to entertain this motion. (See Manhan v. Gallagher (2021) 62 Cal.App.5th 504, 509 ["When a postdismissal or postjudgment motion involves collateral statutory rights, then the court may retain jurisdiction to determine and enforce those rights."]; Pittman v. Beck Park Apartments Ltd. (2018) 20 Cal.App.5th 1009 [holding that the trial court did not lose jurisdiction to declare a plaintiff a vexatious litigant after a voluntary dismissal].)

The motion is denied to the extent plaintiff asserts that she was not properly served with the underlying vexatious litigant motions. On December 27, 2022, the court issued an order finding that those motions were properly served and denied plaintiff's motion to vacate pursuant to CCP 473. This motion is an improper motion for reconsideration of that order. (See CCP 1008.)

Lastly, plaintiff fails to show that it was error for the court to rely on Tina Yan and Thai Ming Chiu v. Joseph Hearst, Alameda Superior Court case no. RG19-017165, in determining that plaintiff is a vexatious litigant within the meaning of CCP 391(b). The record shows that plaintiff "maintained" that action within the meaning of CCP 391(b)(1) after counsel substituted out of the case. (See Fu Decl., Exs. B-G.) Indeed, plaintiff's further unsuccessful appeals are additional examples of vexatious conduct. (See CCP 391(a) [defining litigation]; Garcia v. Lacey (2014) 231 Cal.App.4th 402, 406 ["A litigation includes an appeal or civil writ proceeding filed in an appellate court."].) In sum, plaintiff fails to demonstrate "a material change in facts and that the ends of justice would be served by vacating the order." (CCP 391.8.)

Order signed.

Judge: Suzanne R. Bolanos; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/SRB)

Motion To Set Aside Orders Pursuant To Ccp 473(D)) (Motions)
Off Calendar Dec-23-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF TINA YAN'S Motion To Set Aside Orders Pursuant To Ccp 473(D).

Plaintiff Tina Yan's motion to vacate orders pursuant to CCP 473(d) is denied. Plaintiff fails to maintain her evidentiary burden. Plaintiff contends that she was not served with several motions. But the underlying motions and the Poon declaration demonstrate proper service. Plaintiff fails to file a declaration demonstrating a lack of service. Rather, the only evidence that plaintiff proffers is a declaration from Demas Yan. Demas Yan is a disbarred attorney, who has been declared a vexatious litigant, and he has a history of abusing the court process. (Fu Decl., Exs. 1-2, 4, 5, 6.) The Demas Yan declaration is not credible and fails to show a lack of service.

Proposed order submitted.

Judge: Vedica Puri; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/VP)

ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Oct-24-2022 Dismissal
MINI MINUTES: No appearance. Notice having been given and there being no appearance or objection filed, the Court orders the case dismissed pursuant to CCP 583.210 and CCP 583.250. JUDGE: SAMUEL K. FENG; Clerk: J. Rumsey; Proceedings not reported.

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Awarding Attorney'S Fees And Costs Pursuant To Ccp 425.16(C)(1)
Off Calendar Aug-26-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT BRYANT FU's Motion For Order Awarding Attorney'S Fees And Costs Pursuant To CCP 425.16(C)(1):

Grant. No opposition filed.
